The psalmist’s words were guided by the Spirit of God, just as a pen is guided by the hand of a ready writer. This psalm speaks about King Jesus, his kingdom, and his rule. It is regrettable that this good topic isn't discussed more often. There is more in Christ to inspire our love than there is or can be in any creation. This world and its attractions are eager to draw our hearts away from Christ; therefore, we need to understand how much more worthy he is of our love. By his word, his promise, his gospel, God’s good will is made known to us, and God’s good work is begun and carried on in us. The psalmist, verse #, joyfully predicts the progress and success of the Messiah. The arrows of conviction are very frightening in the hearts of sinners until they are humbled and reconciled; but the arrows of vengeance will be even more so to his enemies who refuse to submit. All who have seen his glory and tasted his grace rejoice to see him, by his word and Spirit, bring enemies and strangers under his rule.

The throne of this almighty King is established forever. While the Holy Spirit leads Christ’s people to look to his cross, he teaches them to see the evil of sin and the beauty of holiness; so that none of them can feel encouraged to continue in sin. The Mediator is God; otherwise, he would not have been able to do the work of the Mediator or fit to wear the crown of the Mediator. God the Father, as his God with respect to his human nature and mediatorial offices, has given to him the Holy Spirit without measure. Thus anointed to be a Prophet, Priest, and King, Christ has preeminence in the gladdening gifts and graces of the spirit, and from his fullness communicates them to his brethren in human nature. The Spirit is called the oil of gladness because of the delight wherewith Christ was filled while carrying on his undertakings. The salvation of sinners is the joy of angels, much more of the Son. And as we are conformed to his holy image, we may expect the gladdening gifts and influences of the Comforter. The excellences of the Messiah, the suitability of his offices, and the sufficiency of his grace seem to be intended by the fragrance of his garments. The church formed of true believers is here compared to the queen, whom, by an everlasting covenant, the Lord Jesus has betrothed to himself. This is the bride, the Lamb’s wife, whose graces are compared to fine linen for their purity; to gold for their costliness: for as we owe our redemption, so we owe our adorning to the precious blood of the Son of God.

If we desire to share these blessings, we must listen to Christ's word. We must forget our carnal and sinful attachments and pursuits. He must be our Lord as well as our Savior; all idols must be thrown away so that we can give him our whole heart. And here is good encouragement to break off from former alliances. The beauty of holiness, both on the church and on particular believers, is, in the sight of Christ, of great price and very amiable. The work of grace is the workmanship of the Spirit; it is the image of Christ upon the soul, a partaking of the Divine nature. It is free of all sin; there is none in it, nor does any come from it. There is nothing glorious in the old man or corrupt nature; but in the new man, or work of grace upon the soul, everything is glorious. The robe of Christ’s righteousness, which he has worked out for his church, the Father imputes to her and bestows upon her. None are brought to Christ except those whom the Father brings. This notes the conversion of souls to him. The robe of righteousness and garments of salvation, the change of raiment Christ has put upon her. Those who strictly cleave to Christ, loving him with a single heart, are companions of the bride, who partake of the very same grace, enjoy the same privileges, and share in one common salvation. These, every one, shall be brought to the King; not one lost or left behind. Instead of the Old Testament church, there will be a New Testament church, a Gentile church. In the believing hope of our everlasting happiness in the other world, let us always keep up the remembrance of Christ as our only way thither and transmit the remembrance of him to succeeding generations so that his name may endure forever.
